summary: Meta-style behavioral mock interview. Andre interviews Johnny, a staff data scientist at British Petroleum with prior ML experience at Meta and blockchain.com. Questions cover ML career journey, technically challenging project (fraud detection ML model at blockchain.com), long-term vision/roadmap (BP Castrol engineering transformation), conflict resolution (senior PM proposing competing platform), project failure and recovery (demand forecasting for BP convenience stores), mentorship, and continuous technical learning. Ends with detailed structured feedback across four dimensions: delivering impact, commitment to learning, resolving conflict, and clear communication.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Hey Johnny. How are you today?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Hello Andre. Yeah, I'm good. I hope you are good as well.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, I'm good. So, today we're doing a behavioral mock interview meta style. We'll go through few common questions and I share detailed feedback in the end. How do you feel about that?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, let's try that.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, sounds good. Let's start right away. The first question is a classic. Tell me about yourself and your journey in machine learning.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So I'm Johnny and I started my career as a software engineer. It was like 11 years ago and then I quickly switched to machine learning which happened 3 years after I started my career because I found machine learning is more interesting area to be in. So I worked in some startups that nobody remembers the name and after that I joined Meta I think five years ago as machine learning engineer. After that I switch — I joined pre-IPO startup blockchain.com, it's a crypto wallet company, one of the biggest one of the oldest in the world, and after that so I worked there as machine learning lead like in machine learning and data engineering, I worked there for a couple of years and then I joined my current company British Petroleum one year and a half ago as a staff data scientist.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Sounds awesome. Thank you for sharing that. Okay, tell me about the most technically challenging project that you've led from start to finish.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, I think I will tell about the project that I did at my previous company blockchain.com. So, the project was to build a machine learning model to predict fraudulent transactions for our bank payments. So when I joined the team, we just compiled the team of data scientists and data engineers and data analysts to tackle fraud and our fraud rates were really high. So fraud rate is the ratio between fraudulent transactions and all transactions and our ratio — so we had from our vendors from Westcard, from Visa, from our bank partner — we had certain threshold that this fraud transactions ratio should be under and we had it above that for areas that I needed to improve. And so I made a proposal after I onboarded — I made the proposal to switch our existing vendor provided machine learning model that we used to assess the transactions to build our in-house model. My proposal was that we can build the model better, we can maintain it better and we will have more control of how to use it. And so after we built that and after we rolled it out, we managed to decrease our fraud rates and we managed to exit the Visa fraud monitoring program and yes and our bank partners also happy that we decreased our fraud rates for them as well.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: So let's come back a little and I have a question about like the moment when you made your proposal to start this project. How actually you did that? How you persuaded product managers to like believe in you that you will actually create the system from scratch that never existed in the company before and did you have any presentation or a road map?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, it's actually a good question because in our company we didn't have the infrastructure to roll out the model — so we didn't have any MLOps and we didn't have any feature store. And the model that we needed to have was a real time model. So it needed to reply in real time and yeah we needed to build the infrastructure for that. So it was a challenge. How I made the proposal — I think I joined in April and I made my proposal I think in the middle of summer after I onboarded properly and understood what we had. And I think I started with evaluating the existing model because we used to use the model but nobody evaluated the actual performance of the model in the company, for several reasons because our data science department was small before I joined and so we didn't have dedicated machine learning people to work with that — the startup itself was smaller before they started growing, like when once they hired me. So I mean we didn't have like just people to look after it. And so I evaluated the performance of that model and I saw that the area under the curve — a specific metric to measure the classification models in machine learning — it was like 65% which is not terrible but it's not — you can fairly say that it's not the greatest model that can predict something. So I mean from just looking at that metric I assumed that the model — I mean also we knew that model from our historical usage of the model, that model had some problems. So I kind of from that I started research. So I spent one or two weeks evaluating what we can build. I showed that the model that we can build with our data would be better. So my model was like having 85 or 89% — the same metric, area under the curve — which was like almost 25%, 20% better. So I spoke about that with my product manager. Obviously, they didn't care about this area under the curve metrics because it doesn't tell anything to business people. So then we switched to like the real number of fraudulent transactions that we would catch if we had our model and that we were not catching with the current model. So that was that and then also I made this proposal request to my manager which was VP of Data at that time and I also created this machine learning design document where I described our existing solution what we have and the new solution that I'm proposing — how many more transactions we would be able to catch and how much more money we would be able to earn. So I didn't have like any like formal presentation or formal meeting where like I persuaded — I mean I was selling this to my stakeholders but I had like periodic one-on-ones with my product manager where we, apart from the model, we also were describing or discussing our other initiatives and yeah I also had my design document where everyone looked at and had some feedback.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah sounds good. Thank you for sharing that. So I have a question like what happened after the project started? Tell me about the team and teamwork. So obviously you were like the lead engineer on the project — how you were actually coordinating the work. How many engineers were involved?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So not that many to be honest. So I was — it was me who were involved in the project and I had like one data engineer that worked on building the features for us — offline features — and I had a machine learning engineer, a junior machine learning engineer, who worked with me and helping me with deploying the model like preparing the code for like actual deployment part. So we had like three people working on that. If we do not take into account — we had a sister team that were building a machine learning framework of MLOps for us. We were like first adopters of their framework. If we go a bit in the future, we were the only ones who were using their framework. But anyway, so we had this sister team that was building the deployment framework for us, the MLOps framework. And they had like four people there. But I wasn't that much involved in this part because we were just the users of the framework.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, sounds good. Yeah. So I have a question about the sister team. So obviously you were not like directly managing those people. You were not like working closely with them as I assume. How do you influence them to do something valuable for your project? Because obviously they're probably working on their projects as well at the same time. So how do you like fight for their time?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So that was a bit of a challenge because so they had their own backlog that they wanted to develop different features for and at that time they were also working on migrating our existing data lake to lakehouse. My data engineer was working closely with them. So it wasn't me like who was working closely with them but my data engineer. And if they had some — for example they didn't have time to work on some feature that we needed — my data engineer from my team kind of helped their team to deliver this feature for them that we required. So basically if we needed some feature and they didn't have time for that we just implemented that feature ourselves — they didn't mind. So I mean some teams they can be very restrictive in terms of if you can push the code into their area but that team they were gladly not like that. So they didn't mind for us to put our code there and we had our quorum of data teams together where we coordinated together our shared backlog. So because anyway if you are even if you are different teams we still need to deliver for the business. So we kind of negotiated the main things at least that should be in a quarter — should be delivered in this half — and my model deployment wasn't put into their backlog like first thing when I proposed that but in the next quarter after I made the proposal they put it in the backlog and we worked on it.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Sounds good. Okay last question about on this story. I want to talk about the outcome. So you mentioned that you had this metric called the fraudulent rate and you were trying to lower that metric. Yeah. So were there any other metrics or ways to actually measure the success of a project?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah. So I mean for the CEO of the company the main concern was that we should be out of this fraud monitoring program from our bank partner which we happily did. So that was the main thing and how the bank partner and our vendors like Visa and Mastercard how they evaluated our performance was — the main metric was the ratio of fraudulent transactions. The one metric that we did care about which wasn't the concern of our partners but we cared about that was approval rate because we wanted to have the number of approved transactions still quite high because we didn't want to create the troubles for our users where you have like lots of rejections for your transactions and also I mean if you fight fraud and like reject everything obviously you'll have zero fraud but you'll not have any transactions right so yeah so we had this guardrail metric that we cared about for approval rate but the main metric was the fraudulent ratio.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Thanks for sharing that. Okay, let's switch gears. Next question is describe a time you defined the road map or some sort of long-term vision for a team or across multiple teams.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Example could be something at BP. So when I joined BP, my team where I worked with it was the data team that supported our business — Castrol company, it's like an arm of BP — we were providers of all data related questions, all data related requests and machine learning as well for Castrol. And when I joined the team, the team was heavily third party workers, not like full-time employees of BP. Historically the team was operating under big influence of business. So nobody was concerned that much about like addressing tech debt, doing things from an engineering perspective, which is right, but the main criteria was just to make business happy and how to make business happy is just to deliver everything fast and dirty right because nobody will care about your code base, nobody will care about maintenance of your work because that is your problem. And because we had like this short-term contractors that worked on the specific projects — they may — I mean it's not I don't blame anyone but it's just because you know that you're working short term you'll deliver your project and then you will be working in another company — obviously you'll try to deliver as fast as you can because A, you save money for the business as well because business doesn't pay more for you because you don't address certain technical debts so you deliver faster for the business which is better for the business from that perspective and also I mean it's not your problem because you wouldn't work on that project after some time. So yeah after I joined the team I like identified these issues and I made the proposal that we need to have — like specific — we need to first of all we can't maintain the big team without having tech leads in each squad because we had like team of like almost 100 people and everyone was managed by like business delivery leads so we didn't have — we had like six squads everyone was managed by business delivery like project manager so we didn't have like tech leads in each squad that people that were like concerned about technical debt and everything like better engineering initiatives. And we also needed to hire more like full-time employees. So my proposal was that we need to address these issues and we need to do certain steps to achieve a better result which was like hire more like full-time employees, assign like technical leads in each squad and train them, support them to like be vocal about like engineering problems, about engineering vision of our solutions and start like compiling the backlog for better engineering initiatives that we can do. And yeah so that was a big vision that I proposed — I didn't do it like from the beginning, I did it like after five or six months I was in the company and we started acting on that. So we hired like 20 I think full-time employees at that time and we have tech leads in each squad, we also assembled like specific team within our business department that is called now asset management team and that team is mainly focused on addressing our infrastructure, data infrastructure and like code related initiatives — so they're not working on business they're just supporting like improving our existing infrastructure. So I mean this team we just set up like two months ago, so it was like a big thing for me because we — I started doing that like one year ago. We are still like have a way to go but we are working there. We did code optimization from our cloud. We reduced our cloud costs now significantly — like 500,000 per year. And yeah so we are doing a lot of good things now and yeah that was I think my story.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: That sounds like a huge project actually. Thank you for sharing that. So my question is how do you actually manage this process? Who were like really managing it? Were you doing it like alone yourself on a high level or were there some other people who were involved?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: That's actually a good question because our hierarchy of management at the company is a bit — so first of all we had several reorgs, two reorgs I think at that time and also my managers changed, so we didn't have like clear hierarchy of who is managing — first when I joined we just had these six squads and I was some like person that's floating around in between them. It wasn't my direct report. So I couldn't say to people like we're doing that, we're doing that, we shouldn't be doing that. So I kind of needed to more like persuade people. So it was like, you know, like soft power. So I just was going around like saying to people we need to do that. I think if we had a better like better infrastructure, we didn't have that we wouldn't have that problem. And I was like mentioning that like to my managers as well. And we have also — we had two different management chains, one from business one from technology, which also like was a challenge because so we had like kind of two report chains for our team. So yeah, it was — it wasn't like I'm directly managing the people and directly like asking people to do certain changes but it was like speaking with my manager, like convincing them, speaking with these people, convincing them and then slightly slowly changing things.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, that sounds really impressive because you basically managed to transition the department and introduce tech leads and technical tasks without being like a manager of those people by just using soft power and persuasion. Interesting. Let's move on. I actually want to ask you about conflicts. This is one of my favorite ones. Please tell me about a time you faced a significant conflict or a disagreement with another tech lead, maybe an engineer or product manager.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I think I will tell about my situation that I had at my previous company blockchain.com. When I joined the company in my first several meetings I had my product manager who was my like partner, was my peer, and then I had the manager of that product manager who was like senior product manager who was also participating in our meetings and suggesting some initiatives that we should be doing. And as I said our main concern was like addressing this fraudulent transactions ratio and we had like certain problems there that we needed to address. And this senior product manager they proposed that instead of directly like trying to find certain rules or building the model that I described, we should be investing our time in building the platform where like people without technical expertise, without knowledge of SQL — because for that particular task you need to know SQL — so without knowing SQL they can go and like with drag and drop create certain rules that would do some kind of a back test on our existing like transactions and would tell us what ratio of the transactions we would have for fraudulent transactions and how many of fraudulent transactions we would catch with that rule and how many good transactions we would block with that rule. So they proposed that we build this platform with UI and non-technical people — first of all product manager — I believe they can go and like with drag and drop create rules and play around to propose certain rules for us that we can implement with the backend engineers with like our assisted team of backend engineers and put it in production and like with that fight fraud. So it was the same — the goal was the same to fight fraud — but not address it directly but through building that platform. And my conflict was that what I tried to explain is that we had — we didn't have like enormous team, we had like five people in team, everyone was having like immediate task that was affecting the fraud rate immediately. So I had a data analyst that worked with our financial team that tried to find these rules already. So I had a person who already was dedicated for that, the time was already dedicated for that, to find these certain rules that will be put in production. So without like — so we had a technical person who was working on that first of all and also we didn't have capacity to like invest into building that platform because it would be like three months of work for like three or four people at least and we had immediate problems with the fraud. So yeah, that was my conflict. I tried to explain that. And then also what I proposed as an interim solution is that if they have any ideas about potential rules that could be created, they could explain to us like — I mean just in English — what rules they want us to create and then we will just investigate that on their behalf with our data. So at first they didn't like that because it was kind of — I think — not the thing that they used to have because I mean I was a new joiner. We were like a new build team and he was a like senior product manager who was handling all of that before we kind of existed in the company. So that was I think the main reason why they had problems with me not accepting the priority. But then I think we negotiated and we came up — yeah so we agreed that we wouldn't build the platform but if any rules they can propose we will gladly like investigate them and put them in production. Yeah.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay. Sounds good. So let's now focus on this part that you said that we negotiated. So I want to hear more about how it actually happened. So first of all who initiated this? Was it a meeting or was it an email discussion?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I mean we had a meeting where — I mean first of — how it started right — so we had a like meeting with our product managers where the senior product manager proposed that we should be doing that. So I was first like expecting my direct peer product managers to say that we don't have like capacity for that. After they didn't do that I kind of started like speaking and saying that my team we do not have any people who could be dedicated for that and we don't have time for that — basically we have like our immediate fires to handle. Then I mean that ended on that note. And then after that like async — not like with the broader forum but just one-on-one in our DM as well — we kind of clicked one-on-one with this person. I explained that — I mean these things that I said — that if they have any proposal we're happy to like take that into investigation but building the platform itself will be like a huge initiative and I'm not saying it's completely worthless but at that time we just don't have capacity for that. So I mean that was the idea. I mean we didn't have — we didn't build the platform anyway but the way I described that was like a bit nicer. Yeah.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay sounds good. Yeah thank you for sharing that. Let's move on. Please tell me about a time a project didn't go as planned. So maybe there was some failure or some problem and how you've overcome it.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So we had a project at BP where we needed to predict demand forecasting for our convenience stores and when I joined the team — I mean so I was — I have my team, this team with Castrol, it was like a spare project, like separate project that was happening in parallel that I was asked to be involved. So I jumped in when I realized that the team wasn't progressing very well. They were working on this project for 4 months when I joined and everything that was done was just research. So they just did like lots of EDAs like describing the data, describing the data set, the problem etc etc but nothing was built. And also the approach that they had for tackling the problem — the machine learning model that they were building — wasn't suited for the whole problem because the problem was that we need to predict the demand for our food products, the products that we have at convenience stores. We had like 300 convenience stores in Australia. Each of convenience store, they have like up to several thousand different products in them. But the approach that the team was following at that time was to build specific machine learning model per like store and SKU per product. So which was — as you can imagine — it's like number of machine learning models that they needed to build at the end was like 300 multiply by several thousand which would be like millions and it wasn't scalable. So yeah so several problems as I said like lots of time was invested in like investigation of data and the model itself wasn't scalable. When I once I joined the team I like I mean I justified all of that. I spoke about that with our business and also we didn't have — another problem — we didn't have a product manager dedicated for that project as well. So I mean — so the main problems once I joined — and then I described the situation, I like I created a document where I described all the situation. And BP is a big organization so to make any change you need to have like several phases of approvals and I asked that we need to have like a product manager dedicated for us. So we need to turn things around and for that we need — because we had only two data scientists assigned for the project that were doing this investigation and research but none of them worked on deployment of machine learning model, none of them worked on like building the features for the machine learning model — so they didn't have that experience at all. So I asked for additional resources — we need like data engineer, we need like engineer who would work on like productionizing the model and we need a product manager. So I got approval for all of that. So we kind of staffed the team with additional people and then I think after three months we made the pilot run for the model. So we rolled it out for like 10 different sites and tested it out and obviously we had some challenges there as well but at least we rolled it out and the project is still ongoing now as far as I know because I mean we had also I was like just spare involvement from me as I said so we had like dedicated data scientists for that project now so they are working on that project like fully at the moment as far as I know and they are like progressing. But at least we turned things around and we delivered some progress. We delivered something totally.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay. Yeah, sounds good. Thank you for sharing that. So I'm actually interested in the thing that you mentioned in the end that you had some like issues with that model and I'm asking because that was the problem that happened after you joined. So it's sort of like you felt the responsibility and then you helped the team somehow to solve this problem. So that's why I want to hear more about this particular case.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So what we had — so after we rolled out our pilot run what we identified is that our data quality — that we assumed — we knew that our data quality is not very good but we assumed it would be good enough. But once we started our pilot run at that specific number of food products that we picked and the sites that we picked — that was the main thing because our data quality overall was like average but for specific sites that we rolled it out it was very bad like — for some of the sites, site per SKU combination, we didn't have data at all — like so if you don't have any data obviously you cannot predict anything. So that was the problem that we identified and it was our mistake that we didn't spot it before our pilot run. We only identified that during pilot run. I mean in our defense we can say that business changed the sides without like taking into account our suggestions because we suggested one set of sites, they picked another one and for that specific sites per SKU we didn't have like data. And we needed to change our model so it at least if we don't have any data it still produces some reasonable result based on the same product sales in different stores, not in this store, right? So we — and that was the thing that we needed to change. So we were changing that during our pilot run and negotiating with the business explaining why we have such bad predictions on the fly. So that was the problem and the business obviously wasn't happy that we didn't support it before but we kind of — we tried to explain that our suggested sites were not picked and we are working with the other sites that we didn't anticipate. And but still it was our mistake — I mean completely was on our data science team that we didn't take into account that and we didn't build our solution robust enough that we can still predict something meaningful while we don't have any data.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: So my question here is was it like an urgent problem or not? Because we're talking about demand prediction for convenience stores and you mentioned that the data was bad. So how does it affect like sales or what was the problem? So was it urgent?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: It was an immediate problem because the products that we rolled it out for were food products that they bake within a day. So how they used our predictions — they needed to know how many food products they need to bake. So how many like different — it's like all — it's like frozen products that you bake within a day and then you sell.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay. Interesting. So that sounds like an urgent problem. So I want to hear what you did like your actions like step by step after the moment you heard that this thing is not working.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Obviously once we figured out that we have this problem that we have like source and SKU-specific SKUs that we have very like out of predictions that not like aligned at all — what I obviously did is like we built the — in one or two days we rolled out the quick fix which was just predicting the average of the sales. So if we don't have anything in the meantime while we were developing the better machine learning approach we rolled out the just quick average of last seven days so they have something to work with in the meantime. So we rolled it out and then I think in a week or in two weeks we like kind of came up with a better approach. So as I said like take into account the sales in different different stores.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yep. Sounds good. Sounds good. All right. Let's move on to the next one. So I want to hear more about your mentorship experience. So tell me about a time you grew or mentored other engineers or researchers maybe machine learning engineers. So how do you approach it? Generally.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: I'm not sure why I have a general approach because people tend to have different personal situations. I can tell about some examples like — my first person that I mentored — I worked in a startup before before Facebook, I worked in a startup as a machine learning engineer, I don't have any reports, I didn't have any reports, I was just machine learning engineer developing the solution for — it was like analyzing tweets and trying to predict stock movement based on tweet sentiment. There's like a social network stock tweets and we tried to predict stock movement based on this tweet sentiment. So I was developing that solution. It was like very very like out of touch — not affecting anything project that I was working with and I liked it very much but yeah doesn't matter. So I worked there and I had like a junior junior engineer who wasn't a machine learning engineer, he was I think backend engineer working at that team that expressed the desire to work with me and with my project. And I accepted that even knowing that they don't have any machine learning experience — I was thinking that they could help me with like productionizing the code to put that model into production and etc etc. So once they joined I was explaining to them what to do like — so I'm basically like explaining machine learning also like in parallel because they didn't have any experience there. And I think once — I also was teaching them what to do, I mean how to engineer, how to tackle specific problems because I remember once sometimes this person — once they had like — once they struggled with something they just gave up and said like, "Oh, I don't know how to do that. I don't know how to fix that." And I was like doing some kind of pair programming. I was opening the code together and like going through the codebase and explaining, okay, I also don't know how to fix that. Let's try to do that together. Right? So we'll have this problem. Let's try to figure out what causes this problem. Let's go over the codebase. So I was just explaining how to like navigate through the code, different codebase. And because we used some library that had some problems we fixed the library in the meantime. And I was explaining that, also I was teaching them yeah I mean coding standards that I found reasonable and etc etc. And also only later I learned that person had serious problems in their previous team and they didn't want to work at that team, they wanted to resign from the company, but they like decided to try with me and I kind of returned them back into like loving software engineering etc etc and now this person works as senior software engineer at Facebook.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay cool thank you for sharing that. Let's move on to our last question. So please tell me about something technical that you learned outside of your immediate work responsibilities.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So I can't say in my last couple of years but before that all my career I think I was like doing some courses in parallel. Now I like don't have — maybe time for that. My last big course that I did was with machine learning when I worked at Facebook. I bought like one year course that you have like participate 20 hours per week I think or something like that so I was doing it for 6 months and I like I finished that course — it's called Hard ML like hard machine learning so by the name you can assume that it was like a hard course. And yeah it was a like interesting course. I learned different areas of machine learning that I didn't have exposure with my experience — like recommendation systems — I never worked with recommendations — I learned that and like uplift modeling when you like need to predict the marketing campaign result. So yeah I mean things that I learned there. And in machine learning there are like different areas. So you could not work on all of them but still you should expect that at some point in your business you can interact with them like it happened to me with these fraudulent transactions like with fraud data right. So you need to know the broad spectrum of applications of machine learning to like to be helpful for the business. So I always tried to do that and actually I just realized I'm doing the machine learning system design lecture course. So I'm like participating there at the moment. So it's happening on Saturdays and Sundays every weekend. We have lectures and some yeah some outside lecture work. So yeah, I think it's just — you need to be — if you want to continue your career at software development and machine learning in particular, you need to be in a loop. You need to know what's happening especially with this like crazy development of AI, of Gen AI and like these new things that is happening every time. So we need to be in sync with the world.

training_pairs.question: Tell me about yourself and your journey in machine learning.
training_pairs.answer: So I'm Johnny and I started my career as a software engineer. It was like 11 years ago and then I quickly switched to machine learning which happened 3 years after I started my career because I found machine learning is more interesting area to be in. So I worked in some startups that nobody remembers the name and after that I joined Meta I think five years ago as machine learning engineer. After that I switch — I joined pre-IPO startup blockchain.com, it's a crypto wallet company, one of the biggest one of the oldest in the world, and after that so I worked there as machine learning lead like in machine learning and data engineering, I worked there for a couple of years and then I joined my current company British Petroleum one year and a half ago as a staff data scientist.
training_pairs.follow_up: Tell me about the most technically challenging project that you've led from start to finish.

training_pairs.question: Tell me about the most technically challenging project that you've led from start to finish.
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, I think I will tell about the project that I did at my previous company blockchain.com. So, the project was to build a machine learning model to predict fraudulent transactions for our bank payments. So when I joined the team, we just compiled the team of data scientists and data engineers and data analysts to tackle fraud and our fraud rates were really high. So fraud rate is the ratio between fraudulent transactions and all transactions and our ratio — so we had from our vendors from Westcard, from Visa, from our bank partner — we had certain threshold that this fraud transactions ratio should be under and we had it above that for areas that I needed to improve. And so I made a proposal after I onboarded — I made the proposal to switch our existing vendor provided machine learning model that we used to assess the transactions to build our in-house model. My proposal was that we can build the model better, we can maintain it better and we will have more control of how to use it. And so after we built that and after we rolled it out, we managed to decrease our fraud rates and we managed to exit the Visa fraud monitoring program and yes and our bank partners also happy that we decreased our fraud rates for them as well.
training_pairs.follow_up: How actually you did that? How you persuaded product managers to like believe in you that you will actually create the system from scratch that never existed in the company before and did you have any presentation or a road map?

training_pairs.question: How did you persuade product managers to believe in you that you would actually create the system from scratch that never existed in the company before? Did you have any presentation or roadmap?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, it's actually a good question because in our company we didn't have the infrastructure to roll out the model — so we didn't have any MLOps and we didn't have any feature store. And the model that we needed to have was a real time model. So it needed to reply in real time and yeah we needed to build the infrastructure for that. So it was a challenge. How I made the proposal — I think I joined in April and I made my proposal I think in the middle of summer after I onboarded properly and understood what we had. And I think I started with evaluating the existing model because we used to use the model but nobody evaluated the actual performance of the model in the company, for several reasons because our data science department was small before I joined and so we didn't have dedicated machine learning people to work with that. And so I evaluated the performance of that model and I saw that the area under the curve — a specific metric to measure the classification models in machine learning — it was like 65% which is not terrible but it's not — you can fairly say that it's not the greatest model that can predict something. So I mean from just looking at that metric I assumed that the model — I mean also we knew that model from our historical usage of the model, that model had some problems. So I kind of from that I started research. So I spent one or two weeks evaluating what we can build. I showed that the model that we can build with our data would be better. So my model was like having 85 or 89% — the same metric, area under the curve — which was like almost 25%, 20% better. So I spoke about that with my product manager. Obviously, they didn't care about this area under the curve metrics because it doesn't tell anything to business people. So then we switched to like the real number of fraudulent transactions that we would catch if we had our model and that we were not catching with the current model. So that was that and then also I made this proposal request to my manager which was VP of Data at that time and I also created this machine learning design document where I described our existing solution what we have and the new solution that I'm proposing — how many more transactions we would be able to catch and how much more money we would be able to earn. So I didn't have like any like formal presentation or formal meeting where like I persuaded — I mean I was selling this to my stakeholders but I had like periodic one-on-ones with my product manager where we, apart from the model, we also were describing or discussing our other initiatives and yeah I also had my design document where everyone looked at and had some feedback.
training_pairs.follow_up: What happened after the project started? Tell me about the team and teamwork. How many engineers were involved?

training_pairs.question: What happened after the project started? Tell me about the team and teamwork. How many engineers were involved and how were you coordinating the work?
training_pairs.answer: So not that many to be honest. So I was — it was me who were involved in the project and I had like one data engineer that worked on building the features for us — offline features — and I had a machine learning engineer, a junior machine learning engineer, who worked with me and helping me with deploying the model like preparing the code for like actual deployment part. So we had like three people working on that. If we do not take into account — we had a sister team that were building a machine learning framework of MLOps for us. We were like first adopters of their framework. If we go a bit in the future, we were the only ones who were using their framework. But anyway, so we had this sister team that was building the deployment framework for us, the MLOps framework. And they had like four people there. But I wasn't that much involved in this part because we were just the users of the framework.
training_pairs.follow_up: You were not like directly managing those people. How do you influence them to do something valuable for your project? How do you like fight for their time?

training_pairs.question: You were not directly managing the sister team and not working closely with them. How did you influence them to do something valuable for your project? How did you fight for their time?
training_pairs.answer: So that was a bit of a challenge because so they had their own backlog that they wanted to develop different features for and at that time they were also working on migrating our existing data lake to lakehouse. My data engineer was working closely with them. So it wasn't me like who was working closely with them but my data engineer. And if they had some — for example they didn't have time to work on some feature that we needed — my data engineer from my team kind of helped their team to deliver this feature for them that we required. So basically if we needed some feature and they didn't have time for that we just implemented that feature ourselves — they didn't mind. So I mean some teams they can be very restrictive in terms of if you can push the code into their area but that team they were gladly not like that. So they didn't mind for us to put our code there and we had our quorum of data teams together where we coordinated together our shared backlog. So because anyway if you are even if you are different teams we still need to deliver for the business. So we kind of negotiated the main things at least that should be in a quarter — should be delivered in this half — and my model deployment wasn't put into their backlog like first thing when I proposed that but in the next quarter after I made the proposal they put it in the backlog and we worked on it.
training_pairs.follow_up: I want to talk about the outcome. Were there any other metrics or ways to actually measure the success of the project?

training_pairs.question: Were there any other metrics or ways to actually measure the success of the project beyond the fraudulent rate?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah. So I mean for the CEO of the company the main concern was that we should be out of this fraud monitoring program from our bank partner which we happily did. So that was the main thing and how the bank partner and our vendors like Visa and Mastercard how they evaluated our performance was — the main metric was the ratio of fraudulent transactions. The one metric that we did care about which wasn't the concern of our partners but we cared about that was approval rate because we wanted to have the number of approved transactions still quite high because we didn't want to create the troubles for our users where you have like lots of rejections for your transactions and also I mean if you fight fraud and like reject everything obviously you'll have zero fraud but you'll not have any transactions right so yeah so we had this guardrail metric that we cared about for approval rate but the main metric was the fraudulent ratio.
training_pairs.follow_up: Describe a time you defined the road map or some sort of long-term vision for a team or across multiple teams.

training_pairs.question: Describe a time you defined the road map or some sort of long-term vision for a team or across multiple teams.
training_pairs.answer: Example could be something at BP. So when I joined BP, my team where I worked with it was the data team that supported our business — Castrol company, it's like an arm of BP — we were providers of all data related questions, all data related requests and machine learning as well for Castrol. And when I joined the team, the team was heavily third party workers, not like full-time employees of BP. Historically the team was operating under big influence of business. So nobody was concerned that much about like addressing tech debt, doing things from an engineering perspective, which is right, but the main criteria was just to make business happy and how to make business happy is just to deliver everything fast and dirty right because nobody will care about your code base, nobody will care about maintenance of your work because that is your problem. And because we had like this short-term contractors that worked on the specific projects — they may — I mean it's not I don't blame anyone but it's just because you know that you're working short term you'll deliver your project and then you will be working in another company — obviously you'll try to deliver as fast as you can because A, you save money for the business as well because business doesn't pay more for you because you don't address certain technical debts so you deliver faster for the business which is better for the business from that perspective and also I mean it's not your problem because you wouldn't work on that project after some time. So yeah after I joined the team I like identified these issues and I made the proposal that we need to have tech leads in each squad because we had like team of like almost 100 people and everyone was managed by like business delivery leads so we didn't have tech leads in each squad that people that were like concerned about technical debt and everything like better engineering initiatives. And we also needed to hire more like full-time employees. So my proposal was that we need to address these issues and we need to do certain steps to achieve a better result which was like hire more like full-time employees, assign like technical leads in each squad and train them, support them to like be vocal about like engineering problems, about engineering vision of our solutions and start like compiling the backlog for better engineering initiatives that we can do. And yeah so that was a big vision that I proposed — I didn't do it like from the beginning, I did it like after five or six months I was in the company and we started acting on that. So we hired like 20 I think full-time employees at that time and we have tech leads in each squad, we also assembled like specific team within our business department that is called now asset management team and that team is mainly focused on addressing our infrastructure, data infrastructure and like code related initiatives — so they're not working on business they're just supporting like improving our existing infrastructure. So I mean this team we just set up like two months ago, so it was like a big thing for me because we — I started doing that like one year ago. We are still like have a way to go but we are working there. We did code optimization from our cloud. We reduced our cloud costs now significantly — like 500,000 per year. And yeah so we are doing a lot of good things now and yeah that was I think my story.
training_pairs.follow_up: How did you actually manage this process? Were you doing it alone yourself on a high level or were there some other people who were involved?

training_pairs.question: How did you actually manage this process? Were you doing it alone or were there other people involved?
training_pairs.answer: That's actually a good question because our hierarchy of management at the company is a bit — so first of all we had several reorgs, two reorgs I think at that time and also my managers changed, so we didn't have like clear hierarchy of who is managing — first when I joined we just had these six squads and I was some like person that's floating around in between them. It wasn't my direct report. So I couldn't say to people like we're doing that, we're doing that, we shouldn't be doing that. So I kind of needed to more like persuade people. So it was like, you know, like soft power. So I just was going around like saying to people we need to do that. I think if we had a better like better infrastructure, we didn't have that we wouldn't have that problem. And I was like mentioning that like to my managers as well. And we have also — we had two different management chains, one from business one from technology, which also like was a challenge because so we had like kind of two report chains for our team. So yeah, it was — it wasn't like I'm directly managing the people and directly like asking people to do certain changes but it was like speaking with my manager, like convincing them, speaking with these people, convincing them and then slightly slowly changing things.
training_pairs.follow_up: Please tell me about a time you faced a significant conflict or a disagreement with another tech lead maybe an engineer or product manager.

training_pairs.question: Please tell me about a time you faced a significant conflict or a disagreement with another tech lead, maybe an engineer or product manager.
training_pairs.answer: I think I will tell about my situation that I had at my previous company blockchain.com. When I joined the company in my first several meetings I had my product manager who was my like partner, was my peer, and then I had the manager of that product manager who was like senior product manager who was also participating in our meetings and suggesting some initiatives that we should be doing. And as I said our main concern was like addressing this fraudulent transactions ratio and we had like certain problems there that we needed to address. And this senior product manager they proposed that instead of directly like trying to find certain rules or building the model that I described, we should be investing our time in building the platform where like people without technical expertise, without knowledge of SQL — because for that particular task you need to know SQL — so without knowing SQL they can go and like with drag and drop create certain rules that would do some kind of a back test on our existing like transactions and would tell us what ratio of the transactions we would have for fraudulent transactions and how many of fraudulent transactions we would catch with that rule and how many good transactions we would block with that rule. So they proposed that we build this platform with UI and non-technical people — first of all product manager — I believe they can go and like with drag and drop create rules and play around to propose certain rules for us that we can implement with the backend engineers with like our assisted team of backend engineers and put it in production and like with that fight fraud. So it was the same — the goal was the same to fight fraud — but not address it directly but through building that platform. And my conflict was that what I tried to explain is that we had — we didn't have like enormous team, we had like five people in team, everyone was having like immediate task that was affecting the fraud rate immediately. So I had a data analyst that worked with our financial team that tried to find these rules already. So I had a person who already was dedicated for that, the time was already dedicated for that, to find these certain rules that will be put in production. So without like — so we had a technical person who was working on that first of all and also we didn't have capacity to like invest into building that platform because it would be like three months of work for like three or four people at least and we had immediate problems with the fraud. So yeah, that was my conflict. I tried to explain that. And then also what I proposed as an interim solution is that if they have any ideas about potential rules that could be created, they could explain to us like — I mean just in English — what rules they want us to create and then we will just investigate that on their behalf with our data. So at first they didn't like that because it was kind of — I think — not the thing that they used to have because I mean I was a new joiner. We were like a new build team and he was a like senior product manager who was handling all of that before we kind of existed in the company. So that was I think the main reason why they had problems with me not accepting the priority. But then I think we negotiated and we came up — yeah so we agreed that we wouldn't build the platform but if any rules they can propose we will gladly like investigate them and put them in production.
training_pairs.follow_up: You said that we negotiated. I want to hear more about how it actually happened. Who initiated this? Was it a meeting or was it an email discussion?

training_pairs.question: You said that you negotiated. How did it actually happen? Who initiated it — was it a meeting or an email discussion?
training_pairs.answer: I mean we had a meeting where — I mean first of — how it started right — so we had a like meeting with our product managers where the senior product manager proposed that we should be doing that. So I was first like expecting my direct peer product managers to say that we don't have like capacity for that. After they didn't do that I kind of started like speaking and saying that my team we do not have any people who could be dedicated for that and we don't have time for that — basically we have like our immediate fires to handle. Then I mean that ended on that note. And then after that like async — not like with the broader forum but just one-on-one in our DM as well — we kind of clicked one-on-one with this person. I explained that — I mean these things that I said — that if they have any proposal we're happy to like take that into investigation but building the platform itself will be like a huge initiative and I'm not saying it's completely worthless but at that time we just don't have capacity for that. So I mean that was the idea. I mean we didn't have — we didn't build the platform anyway but the way I described that was like a bit nicer.
training_pairs.follow_up: Please tell me about a time a project didn't go as planned. Maybe there was some failure or some problem and how you've overcome it.

training_pairs.question: Please tell me about a time a project didn't go as planned — maybe there was some failure or problem and how you overcame it.
training_pairs.answer: So we had a project at BP where we needed to predict demand forecasting for our convenience stores and when I joined the team — I mean so I was — I have my team, this team with Castrol, it was like a spare project, like separate project that was happening in parallel that I was asked to be involved. So I jumped in when I realized that the team wasn't progressing very well. They were working on this project for 4 months when I joined and everything that was done was just research. So they just did like lots of EDAs like describing the data, describing the data set, the problem etc etc but nothing was built. And also the approach that they had for tackling the problem — the machine learning model that they were building — wasn't suited for the whole problem because the problem was that we need to predict the demand for our food products, the products that we have at convenience stores. We had like 300 convenience stores in Australia. Each of convenience store, they have like up to several thousand different products in them. But the approach that the team was following at that time was to build specific machine learning model per like store and SKU per product. So which was — as you can imagine — it's like number of machine learning models that they needed to build at the end was like 300 multiply by several thousand which would be like millions and it wasn't scalable. So yeah so several problems as I said like lots of time was invested in like investigation of data and the model itself wasn't scalable. When I once I joined the team I like I mean I justified all of that. I spoke about that with our business and also we didn't have — another problem — we didn't have a product manager dedicated for that project as well. So I mean — so the main problems once I joined — and then I described the situation, I like I created a document where I described all the situation. And BP is a big organization so to make any change you need to have like several phases of approvals and I asked that we need to have like a product manager dedicated for us. So we need to turn things around and for that we need — because we had only two data scientists assigned for the project that were doing this investigation and research but none of them worked on deployment of machine learning model, none of them worked on like building the features for the machine learning model — so they didn't have that experience at all. So I asked for additional resources — we need like data engineer, we need like engineer who would work on like productionizing the model and we need a product manager. So I got approval for all of that. So we kind of staffed the team with additional people and then I think after three months we made the pilot run for the model. So we rolled it out for like 10 different sites and tested it out and obviously we had some challenges there as well but at least we rolled it out and the project is still ongoing now as far as I know because I mean we had also I was like just spare involvement from me as I said so we had like dedicated data scientists for that project now so they are working on that project like fully at the moment as far as I know and they are like progressing. But at least we turned things around and we delivered some progress.
training_pairs.follow_up: I'm actually interested in the issues you mentioned — that was the problem that happened after you joined. Can you tell me more about this particular case?

training_pairs.question: Tell me more about the issues that happened during the pilot run — the problem that you had to solve after you joined.
training_pairs.answer: So what we had — so after we rolled out our pilot run what we identified is that our data quality — that we assumed — we knew that our data quality is not very good but we assumed it would be good enough. But once we started our pilot run at that specific number of food products that we picked and the sites that we picked — that was the main thing because our data quality overall was like average but for specific sites that we rolled it out it was very bad like — for some of the sites, site per SKU combination, we didn't have data at all — like so if you don't have any data obviously you cannot predict anything. So that was the problem that we identified and it was our mistake that we didn't spot it before our pilot run. We only identified that during pilot run. I mean in our defense we can say that business changed the sides without like taking into account our suggestions because we suggested one set of sites, they picked another one and for that specific sites per SKU we didn't have like data. And we needed to change our model so it at least if we don't have any data it still produces some reasonable result based on the same product sales in different stores, not in this store, right? So we — and that was the thing that we needed to change. So we were changing that during our pilot run and negotiating with the business explaining why we have such bad predictions on the fly. So that was the problem and the business obviously wasn't happy that we didn't support it before but we kind of — we tried to explain that our suggested sites were not picked and we are working with the other sites that we didn't anticipate. And but still it was our mistake — I mean completely was on our data science team that we didn't take into account that and we didn't build our solution robust enough that we can still predict something meaningful while we don't have any data.
training_pairs.follow_up: Was it like an urgent problem or not? How does it affect sales?

training_pairs.question: Was it an urgent problem? How did it affect sales?
training_pairs.answer: It was an immediate problem because the products that we rolled it out for were food products that they bake within a day. So how they used our predictions — they needed to know how many food products they need to bake. So how many like different — it's like all — it's like frozen products that you bake within a day and then you sell.
training_pairs.follow_up: So I want to hear what you did like your actions like step by step after the moment you heard that this thing is not working.

training_pairs.question: What were your actions step by step after the moment you found out the predictions were badly off?
training_pairs.answer: Obviously once we figured out that we have this problem that we have like source and SKU-specific SKUs that we have very like out of predictions that not like aligned at all — what I obviously did is like we built the — in one or two days we rolled out the quick fix which was just predicting the average of the sales. So if we don't have anything in the meantime while we were developing the better machine learning approach we rolled out the just quick average of last seven days so they have something to work with in the meantime. So we rolled it out and then I think in a week or in two weeks we like kind of came up with a better approach. So as I said like take into account the sales in different different stores.
training_pairs.follow_up: Tell me about a time you grew or mentored other engineers or researchers. How do you approach it?

training_pairs.question: Tell me about a time you grew or mentored other engineers or researchers. How do you approach it?
training_pairs.answer: I'm not sure why I have a general approach because people tend to have different personal situations. I can tell about some examples like — my first person that I mentored — I worked in a startup before before Facebook, I worked in a startup as a machine learning engineer, I don't have any reports, I didn't have any reports, I was just machine learning engineer developing the solution for — it was like analyzing tweets and trying to predict stock movement based on tweet sentiment. There's like a social network stock tweets and we tried to predict stock movement based on this tweet sentiment. So I was developing that solution. It was like very very like out of touch — not affecting anything project that I was working with and I liked it very much but yeah doesn't matter. So I worked there and I had like a junior junior engineer who wasn't a machine learning engineer, he was I think backend engineer working at that team that expressed the desire to work with me and with my project. And I accepted that even knowing that they don't have any machine learning experience — I was thinking that they could help me with like productionizing the code to put that model into production and etc etc. So once they joined I was explaining to them what to do like — so I'm basically like explaining machine learning also like in parallel because they didn't have any experience there. And I think once — I also was teaching them what to do, I mean how to engineer, how to tackle specific problems because I remember once sometimes this person — once they had like — once they struggled with something they just gave up and said like, "Oh, I don't know how to do that. I don't know how to fix that." And I was like doing some kind of pair programming. I was opening the code together and like going through the codebase and explaining, okay, I also don't know how to fix that. Let's try to do that together. Right? So we'll have this problem. Let's try to figure out what causes this problem. Let's go over the codebase. So I was just explaining how to like navigate through the code, different codebase. And because we used some library that had some problems we fixed the library in the meantime. And I was explaining that, also I was teaching them yeah I mean coding standards that I found reasonable and etc etc. And also only later I learned that person had serious problems in their previous team and they didn't want to work at that team, they wanted to resign from the company, but they like decided to try with me and I kind of returned them back into like loving software engineering etc etc and now this person works as senior software engineer at Facebook.
training_pairs.follow_up: Please tell me about something technical that you learned outside of your immediate work responsibilities.

training_pairs.question: Please tell me about something technical that you learned outside of your immediate work responsibilities.
training_pairs.answer: So I can't say in my last couple of years but before that all my career I think I was like doing some courses in parallel. Now I like don't have — maybe time for that. My last big course that I did was with machine learning when I worked at Facebook. I bought like one year course that you have like participate 20 hours per week I think or something like that so I was doing it for 6 months and I like I finished that course — it's called Hard ML like hard machine learning so by the name you can assume that it was like a hard course. And yeah it was a like interesting course. I learned different areas of machine learning that I didn't have exposure with my experience — like recommendation systems — I never worked with recommendations — I learned that and like uplift modeling when you like need to predict the marketing campaign result. So yeah I mean things that I learned there. And in machine learning there are like different areas. So you could not work on all of them but still you should expect that at some point in your business you can interact with them like it happened to me with these fraudulent transactions like with fraud data right. So you need to know the broad spectrum of applications of machine learning to like to be helpful for the business. So I always tried to do that and actually I just realized I'm doing the machine learning system design lecture course. So I'm like participating there at the moment. So it's happening on Saturdays and Sundays every weekend. We have lectures and some yeah some outside lecture work. So yeah, I think it's just — you need to be — if you want to continue your career at software development and machine learning in particular, you need to be in a loop. You need to know what's happening especially with this like crazy development of AI, of Gen AI and like these new things that is happening every time. So we need to be in sync with the world.
